title: Oil Heads for Fourth Weekly Loss as Hormuz Clears, Russia Imports Indian Petrol, and Europe Races the Clock on Gas Storage
subhead: Brent nears $71 as analysts slash forecasts for the first time since the Iran war began — but the post-Hormuz calm conceals a scramble: Europe is only a third full on gas with 146 days to winter, Russia is buying petrol from India, and shadow fleet vessels are launching drones at NATO nuclear sites.

sections.heading: Hormuz Clears, Oil Slides — and the Market Prices a Glut
sections.body:
Oil is heading for its fourth consecutive weekly loss, with Brent hovering near $71/barrel after briefly dipping below that level in the previous session. The slide follows the conclusion of US-Iran talks in Doha and accelerating tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.

According to Kpler data cited by CNBC, Saudi Arabia has shipped approximately 34 million barrels of oil through Hormuz since June 17 — more than double the 15 million barrels that transited between March 9 and June 17. Eleven supertankers entered the Gulf between June 23 and July 1, with eight already loaded and five having exited the strait. Kpler analyst Jashan Prema described the revival as 'Saudi crude flows inside the Gulf reviving after months of conflict-driven rerouting.' About 17 million barrels of pre-war Saudi oil remain backed up in the Gulf, according to Kpler.

UBS cut its 2026 and 2027 oil price forecasts on Thursday as Hormuz flows recovered, following a Reuters poll showing analysts had reduced their 2026 price projections for the first time since the Iran war began. Reuters commentary described a 'chaotic rebalancing act' as floating storage crude returns to the market while buyers have already covered their summer requirements.

Despite the recovery, Hormuz remains below pre-crisis throughput. Windward data showed roughly 8.5 million barrels per day passing through on Wednesday, versus nearly 15 million bpd in 2025, according to the US Energy Information Administration. Outbursts of hostilities between the US and Iran continued last week — Tehran attacked two commercial ships and the US responded with strikes — but tanker traffic dipped only temporarily, falling to 8 vessels on Sunday before recovering to 16 by Wednesday.
sections.whyItMatters: The market is rapidly pricing in a supply surge that may overshoot demand. Asian refiners covered their summer needs at premium prices during the crisis; now they have no appetite for prompt cargoes. The rebalancing is messy — too much oil arriving at once into a market where buyers are already stocked.

sections.heading: UAE Rewrites Pricing After OPEC Exit, Targets Asian Buyers
sections.body:
ADNOC is transitioning the Official Selling Prices for its three offshore crude grades — Upper Zakum, Das, and Umm Lulu — from a Murban futures differential to a Dubai-linked formula, OilPrice.com reports. The shift corrects a pricing distortion: during the Iran war, Murban futures surged on extreme backwardation and light-end demand, making medium-sour offshore grades artificially expensive relative to their physical fundamentals.

The move follows the UAE's historic departure from OPEC in late April. Free of production caps that held output to 3.2-3.5 million bpd, the UAE is projected to increase total output to 5.0 million bpd by 2027, per IEA forecasts — an immediate year-on-year increase of 730,000 bpd.

ADNOC is also leveraging the Habshan-Fujairah pipeline to bypass Hormuz entirely, giving it a structural routing advantage over Gulf competitors still dependent on the strait. During the crisis, Asian refiners absorbed at least 30 million barrels of emergency ADNOC crude — India bought roughly 6 million barrels, Japan's Eneos took 3 million, and South Korean refiners SK Energy and GS Energy secured 8 million between them. Those buyers now have full tanks and are demanding discounts on Dubai-linked cargoes.

China's independent refiners, meanwhile, are snapping up cheaper Middle Eastern crude as prices slide, Bloomberg reports.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE is no longer playing by OPEC rules and is restructuring its entire pricing architecture to win long-term Asian market share. The Dubai-linked pricing shift is not a temporary adjustment — it is the new normal for a post-OPEC Gulf producer competing aggressively on price.

sections.heading: Europe's Gas Storage at Five-Year Low, 146 Days to Winter Deadline
sections.body:
European gas storage stands at 49% as of end of June, a five-year low for the date and 14.8 percentage points below the five-year average, according to Gas Infrastructure Europe data compiled by TASS. Net injection in June hit a two-year low of 10.1 billion cubic meters, down 19% year-on-year, while withdrawals totaled 727 million cubic meters.

The Kyiv Independent reports that Europe has only about 146 days until the November 1 target to reach 90% storage capacity. At current injection rates, the Oxford Institute for Energy Studies projects the bloc will only reach roughly 70% by November 1 — well short of the 90% target and the thinnest winter cushion since 2022.

The shortfall stems directly from the Hormuz crisis, which cut off Qatari and Emirati LNG — nearly a fifth of global LNG trade — for roughly four months. That represents approximately 8 bcm of missed supply during the prime injection season. Reopening Hormuz stops the monthly bleeding but cannot recover what was already lost.

Naftogaz's Mykola Kolisnyk argues that the EU's current model — relying on market incentives to fill storage — is failing because the summer-winter price spread has collapsed, removing traders' incentive to inject. Germany's grid operator association FNB Gas has proposed a 'combination model': a 24 TWh strategic reserve plus a 63 TWh supplier filling obligation, held by a central body and released only in crisis. Austria and Hungary already operate dedicated state-controlled gas reserves outside normal market operations.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe is heading into next winter with the thinnest gas cushion in four years, and the market mechanism that traditionally fills storage has broken down. The debate is shifting from whether to intervene to how fast — and whether Ukraine's war-tested model of mandated reserves becomes the EU standard.

sections.heading: US LNG Diverted From Europe to Asia and Africa
sections.body:
For the first time in nearly two years, less than half of US LNG exports went to Europe last month, Reuters reports, citing LSEG data. Stronger Asian prices and record Egyptian imports are diverting cargoes away from the continent that most needs them.

European LNG inflows fell to a ten-month low in June, down 13% from May and 18% below June 2025, according to TASS calculations based on GIE data. While January-June 2026 LNG inflows remained at a record roughly 72.7 bcm, growth slowed to just 1% above the same period in 2025.

European gas futures have been rising on a combination of low storage, the Hormuz overhang, and a heatwave driving cooling demand — but the price signal has not been strong enough to pull LNG cargoes back from Asia, where spot premiums remain higher.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's gas problem is not just about supply that was lost during Hormuz — it is about supply that is now choosing to go elsewhere. Unless European prices rise enough to outbid Asian buyers, the LNG needed to fill storage simply will not arrive.

sections.heading: Russia Imports Indian Petrol as Ukrainian Drone Strikes Cripple Refineries
sections.body:
In a striking reversal for one of the world's largest energy exporters, Russia has begun importing gasoline from India to address domestic fuel shortages caused by Ukrainian drone attacks on its refineries. At least 60,000 metric tonnes have been shipped via sea routes, with two tankers carrying 30,000-40,000 tonnes each currently en route, Republic World reports citing Reuters.

Russia is reportedly seeking to import approximately 400,000 metric tonnes of gasoline per month from India and Belarus combined. Belarus has already increased rail fuel exports to Russia. Putin has publicly acknowledged the shortage, and Russia's parliament approved tax code amendments to subsidize fuel imports.

The crisis traces directly to Ukraine's drone campaign: roughly a third of Russia's refining capacity has been knocked out, causing petrol shortages, rationing, and record prices across Russian cities. In the grain belt, Reuters reports farmers fear they will be unable to harvest crops as fuel supplies dry up.

The India-Russia fuel trade has a circular quality: India is importing record volumes of discounted Russian crude — over half its total crude imports in June — refining it into petrol, and now shipping some of it back to Russia.
sections.whyItMatters: One of the world's biggest oil producers is now a net petrol importer. The drone campaign has done more than knock out refineries — it has reversed the direction of refined product trade between Russia and India, creating an absurd loop where Russian crude goes east, gets refined, and comes back as petrol.

sections.heading: Shadow Fleet Escalation: Armed Tankers and Drones Over NATO Nuclear Sites
sections.body:
Russia's shadow fleet has moved fromsanctions evasion to active intelligence operations. Multiple reports — including from LBC, the Daily News, and the Kyiv Independent — confirm that shadow fleet tankers were used to launch drones over NATO nuclear and military installations in Britain, targeting RAF Lakenheath, Mildenhall, and Fairford. A sanctioned tanker near Hull served as a launch platform, according to intelligence sources cited by LBC.

MSN reports that Russia has armed at least one shadow fleet tanker in response to European interceptions of its vessels. The Daily News describes a broader Russian drone campaign across Europe that 'repeatedly disrupted civilian aviation' while monitoring military sites.

The Glasgow-based Seapeak Maritime came under scrutiny after the Rudolf Samoylovich, a Bahamas-flagged Arc7 LNG tanker on Ukraine's sanctions list, docked at Denmark's Fayard shipyard on June 30 for maintenance. The vessel is one of 35 foreign-flagged ships authorized by the Kremlin to transport LNG from the Arctic port of Sabetta. Up to six Arc7 vessels may need servicing before new EU restrictions on Russian LNG shipping services take effect in January 2027. Each Arc7 vessel has carried an average of 5.3 million tonnes of Russian LNG since the invasion, representing roughly €4 billion in cargo value per ship, according to Urgewald.

Separately, Europe is taking tougher action against tankers falsely flying the Cameroonian flag, Reuters reports, part of a wider crackdown on flags of convenience used by the shadow fleet.

Ukraine has formally argued that shadow fleet vessels should be considered legitimate military targets, according to the Kyiv Independent — a position that, if adopted, would fundamentally alter the legal status of these ships under international maritime law.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet is no longer just a sanctions-busting logistics network. Moscow is weaponizing it as a surveillance and potentially strike platform against NATO territory. The debate over whether these vessels are legitimate military targets could reshape how the West handles Russia's maritime sanctions evasion.

sections.heading: Canada's Tanker Ban Holds as Carney Balances Alberta and BC
sections.body:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney confirmed that a tanker ban on British Columbia's north coast will remain in place under a new federal-provincial agreement with BC, even as Canada pursues a new pipeline to carry Alberta oil to the Pacific. The deal preserves the existing moratorium while the government works toward a separate corridor for Alberta crude, Reuters and AP report.

The announcement is a careful balancing act: Carney needs Alberta's political support and the economic case for Pacific-bound crude, but faces entrenched opposition from BC's government, Indigenous communities, and environmental groups. The tanker ban, originally introduced as a moratorium in 1972 and formalized under Justin Trudeau, has long been a flashpoint in federal-provincial energy politics.
sections.whyItMatters: Carney is trying to thread a needle that has defeated multiple Canadian prime ministers: getting Alberta oil to Pacific markets without running tankers through BC's ecologically sensitive northern waters. The tanker ban holds for now, but the real fight — over a specific pipeline route — has barely begun.

bottomLine: The post-Hormuz energy landscape is settling into an uncomfortable new shape. Oil is sliding toward $71 as supply floods back into a market where buyers are already stocked — but the calm is deceptive. Europe faces a genuine winter gas storage crisis with only 146 days and no market incentive to fill tanks. Russia, an energy superpower, is now importing Indian petrol because Ukraine burned its refineries. The shadow fleet has graduated from sanctions evasion to launching drones at NATO nuclear sites. And the UAE, newly freed from OPEC, is rewriting the pricing architecture of Middle Eastern crude to lock in Asian market share. The war reshaped everything; the 'return to normal' is a different normal.
sources: CNBC, Kpler, Reuters, UBS, OilPrice.com, Bloomberg, TASS, Gas Infrastructure Europe, Oxford Institute for Energy Studies, Kyiv Independent, Naftogaz of Ukraine, FNB Gas, Republic World/Reuters, LBC, MSN, Daily News, Yahoo News, Herald Scotland, Urgewald, AP
